items.title: Variance
items.description: Variance is the other main term in the decomposition — error from predictions changing across training samples. Bias and variance trade off as model complexity changes; this topic owns bias, variance owns sensitivity to data.

items.title: Underfitting vs overfitting
items.description: High bias is the theoretical cause of underfitting (poor train and val error). High variance drives overfitting (low train, high val gap). Diagnosis and fixes are detailed in the underfitting vs overfitting topic.

items.title: Linear regression
items.description: Ordinary linear regression is a low-flexibility model — often high bias on nonlinear data. It illustrates how a simple hypothesis class can systematically miss curved relationships.

items.title: Generalization
items.description: Generalization error includes bias², variance, and irreducible noise. High bias caps generalization even with infinite data because the model class cannot represent f* — see generalization for broader train/test and distribution-shift context.

items.title: Model complexity
items.description: Increasing model complexity (depth, width, features) typically lowers bias until diminishing returns; the model complexity topic maps capacity knobs to the bias–variance curve.

items.title: Expectation and variance
items.description: Bias and variance in the decomposition are defined using E[·] and Var(·) of predictions across training sets — the probability foundations live in expectation and variance.
